one_line_takeaway: Building knowledge-graph-derived multi-hop probes directly from a model's own pre-unlearning knowledge shows that all 12 tested unlearning methods (GA, DPO, NPO and regularized/prompt-based variants) on LLaMA-3.1-8B leave substantially more residual knowledge accessible as query complexity increases (20.1% -> 27.0% -> 33.9% average accuracy from 1-hop to 3-hop), traceable via PatchScopes to unlearning disrupting only the dominant single-hop activation pathway while alternate multi-hop pathways remain intact.
key_finding: On RWKU with LLaMA-3.1-Instruct-8B (Table 2), the target (pre-unlearning) model scores 98.6/97.2/84.1 accuracy on 1-/2-/3-hop forget probes and 93.3 average forget score, while all 12 unlearning methods still leak substantial residual knowledge under multi-hop probing: averaged across all methods, multi-hop query accuracy remains 20.1% at 1-hop, 27.0% at 2-hop, and 33.9% at 3-hop -- i.e., residual-knowledge recovery INCREASES with query complexity, the paper's central novel finding. ULD achieves the best overall trade-off (Overall score RWKU=80.2%, TOFU=78.5%), while Task Vector (TV) has the weakest forgetting (Avg. Multi-hop Forget Score 42.3, worst among all methods) despite strong retention (82.3). ICL preserves general relationship knowledge well (Rel. Ret. RWKU=93.4%, TOFU=87.2%) but suffers a large drop on facts 1-hop away from the forgotten entity (RWKU=34.2%, TOFU=31.5%). Regularization (GDR/KLR) improves average retention score for GA/DPO/NPO by roughly 18-20 percentage points on RWKU and 10-15 points on TOFU relative to unregularized variants. Coverage: the auto-generated probes recover ~78% of RWKU's and ~66% of TOFU's existing Q&A pairs without manual curation or external corpora. Ranking agreement: Spearman correlation between the paper's Removal Criteria and prior benchmark rankings is 0.87 (RWKU) and -0.79 (TOFU, sign flip reflects opposite metric direction), and for Retention Criteria 0.75 (RWKU) and 0.58 (TOFU), all reported significant (p<0.01 or p<0.005). Perturbation study (Table 3, 2-hop queries): substituting intermediate entities with known aliases increases average residual-knowledge recovery by 2.4 percentage points across methods; chain-of-thought decomposition of multi-hop queries produces negligible additional recovery. Proximity effect: retention accuracy for facts 1-hop away from the unlearning target (RWKU=61.7%, TOFU=61.7%) is lower than for facts 2-hops away (RWKU=67.7%, TOFU=66.9%), i.e. unintended forgetting is worse for facts closer to the target. PatchScopes analysis shows single-hop queries resolve the target entity via a single dominant pathway in intermediate transformer layers (easily disrupted by unlearning), whereas two-hop queries show a bifurcated resolution -- the first-hop entity resolves early (layers 1-11) and the second-hop entity resolves later (layers 12-32) -- providing a mechanistic candidate explanation for why unlearning methods, which target dominant single-hop pathways, fail to remove knowledge accessible via alternate multi-hop pathways.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a dynamic, model-specific evaluation framework for LLM unlearning that (1) builds a knowledge graph for a target entity by recursively eliciting facts directly from the pre-unlearning model itself (via breadth-first search with an exponential decay factor alpha on the number of expanded nodes per depth, plus LLM-based alias resolution), rather than drawing on externally curated or GPT-4-generated test sets, and (2) uses that graph to auto-generate single-hop, multi-hop (up to 3 hops), and alias-perturbed probe queries that are used to stress-test a model after an unlearning method has been applied. The authors define three scoring metrics that are original to this paper: Avg. Multi-hop (mean accuracy across 1-/2-/3-hop forget probes, lower is better), Avg. Retention Score (mean accuracy on 1-hop-away fact retention, 2-hop-away fact retention, and relationship retention, higher is better), and an Overall score computed as the harmonic mean of (1 - Avg. Multi-hop) and Avg. Retention Score. They instantiate the framework on 12 unlearning methods (GA, GA+GDR, GA+KLR, DPO, DPO+GDR, DPO+KLR, NPO, NPO+GDR, NPO+KLR, ULD, Task Vector, and In-Context Unlearning/ICL) applied to LLaMA-3.1-Instruct-8B (plus follow-up runs on Phi-4-mini-instruct-3.8B and Granite-3.2-8B-Instruct) using entities drawn from the RWKU and TOFU benchmarks, and additionally run a PatchScopes-based activation-localization analysis comparing where single-hop vs. two-hop entity resolution occurs across transformer layers.
threat_model: This is a benchmark/evaluation-methodology paper, not an attack paper against a deployed system, so it does not define an adversary in the classic red-team sense. The closest analogue to a threat model, stated explicitly in the paper's Discussion, is: unlearning is meant to satisfy a 'right to be forgotten'/safety obligation, and the paper's own framing treats any user (not necessarily malicious) who phrases a request indirectly -- via multi-hop compositional questions, entity aliasing, or paraphrase -- as capable of recovering information an unlearning method was supposed to have erased. The paper explicitly rejects a purely adversarial framing, arguing (Discussion, Sec 6) that 'what matters is whether the sensitive or protected content is fully inaccessible' under any minor query variation, not just under adversarial probing -- i.e., their probes are meant to model realistic downstream use (search assistants, RAG, conversational agents) rather than a sophisticated attacker. The paper explicitly notes it covers only 'a subset of adversarial attacks' (Table 1 footnote) and does not claim to be a general jailbreak or extraction-attack benchmark.
limitations: The authors state these limitations themselves (Sec 6, Discussion and Limitations): (1) knowledge elicitation is easy for well-known/high-salience entities but difficult for low-salience or expert-level domains (they cite WMDP-style content such as novel protein compounds or cybersecurity threats as an example where the target model struggles to produce consistent outputs), creating a 'chicken-and-egg' problem since the method needs successful elicitation to demonstrate unlearning failure via multi-hop probing, yet elicitation itself is the bottleneck for exactly the hard-to-elicit knowledge that matters most; (2) when forget and retain sets overlap substantially (their example: MUSE-Books' need to distinguish copyrighted material from derivative fan works), elicitation-only graph construction is insufficient and requires external knowledge sources or manual disambiguation; (3) the derived metrics are, like any evaluation measure, only an approximation of true unlearning efficacy; (4) the constructed knowledge graph is non-deterministic and, once built, remains static per evaluation rather than adapting adversarially over time; (5) the framework tests unintended forgetting on graph-adjacent knowledge but does not represent general model utility on generic downstream tasks. Additionally, the paper covers only 'a subset of adversarial attacks' per their own Table 1 annotation, and does not include Knowledge Manipulation (MCQ-style) probes, though they note the framework could be extended to support this.

datasets_used: RWKU (Real-World Knowledge Unlearning benchmark, Jin et al. 2025) — 200 unlearning targets, 13,131 forget probes, used as the primary entity/seed source and for coverage and rank-correlation comparisons
TOFU (Task of Fictitious Unlearning, Maini et al. 2024) — 200 unlearning targets, 4,000 forget probes, used for coverage and rank-correlation comparisons
Synthetic retain corpus constructed by the authors from Wikipedia outbound hyperlinks of RWKU targets, since RWKU does not provide an explicit retain corpus
quantitative_results: Coverage: auto-generated probes recover ~78% of RWKU Q&A pairs and ~66% of TOFU Q&A pairs without manual curation
RWKU/LLaMA-3.1-8B multi-hop leakage averaged over all 12 methods: 1-hop=20.1%, 2-hop=27.0%, 3-hop=33.9% (target/pre-unlearning model: 98.6%/97.2%/84.1%)
Best overall trade-off: ULD, Overall score RWKU=80.2%, TOFU=78.5%
Worst forgetting: Task Vector (TV), Avg. Multi-hop Forget Score=42.3 (RWKU, LLaMA-3.1-8B)
ICL: Rel. Ret. RWKU=93.4%, TOFU=87.2%, but 1-hop-away fact retention drops to RWKU=34.2%, TOFU=31.5%
Regularization (GDR/KLR) improves average retention score for GA/DPO/NPO by ~18-20 points (RWKU) and ~10-15 points (TOFU) versus unregularized versions
Spearman rank correlation with prior benchmark rankings: Removal Criteria RWKU=0.87 (p<0.005), TOFU=-0.79 (p<0.005); Retention Criteria RWKU=0.75 (p<0.005), TOFU=0.58 (p<0.01)
Cross-model check on RWKU (Phi-4-mini-instruct 3.8B and Granite-3.2-8B-Instruct): Removal Criteria Spearman=0.88 (p<0.005), Retention Criteria Spearman=0.77 (p<0.005)

Aliasing intermediate entities in 2-hop queries increases average residual-knowledge recovery by +2.4 percentage points (Table 3); chain-of-thought decomposition of multi-hop queries yields negligible additional recovery
Proximity effect: 1-hop-away fact retention (RWKU=61.7%, TOFU=61.7%) lower than 2-hop-away fact retention (RWKU=67.7%, TOFU=66.9%)
Knowledge graph construction under alpha=0.8: average nodes per seed entity at depths 1/2/3 ≈ 57.6/103.7/140.5 on RWKU; total model calls per entity range 228-1,942
baselines_compared: Gradient Ascent (GA) and regularized variants GA+GDR, GA+KLR
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and regularized variants DPO+GDR, DPO+KLR
Negative Preference Optimization (NPO) and regularized variants NPO+GDR, NPO+KLR
Prior benchmark metrics: RWKU's Forget Set / Neighbor Set scores and TOFU's own removal/retention criteria (used for coverage and Spearman rank-correlation comparison, not as competing methods)
